text: We report a case of reversal omegaloop gastric bypass into normal anatomy by robotic approach in a patient who presented malnutrition after omegaloop gastric bypass, also called mini gastric bypass. A sixty nine year old female who had undergone laparoscopic omegaloop gastric bypass in March, uh, twenty forty. She had obstructive sleep apnea and type two diabetes. In September, she had lost about a kilo with persistent diarrhea and a laboratory test showed hypoalbuminemia and anemia. The weight loss curve is shown in this graph. After a period of parenteral nutrition, she had improved her nutritional status.

The surgical team prepares for a robotic reversal of omega loop gastric bypass, positioning six trocars: a 12 mm port for the camera, two 8 mm ports for the robotic arms, two 12 mm ports for stapling and assistance, and a 5 mm port for liver retraction. The robotic system is docked at the patient's left shoulder.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis on the left lobe of the liver, exposing the gastro-jejunostomy. The gastrogenostomy is transected using purple staples. Gastric continuity is restored through a linear staple side-to-side anastomosis between the proximal gastric pouch and the excluded stomach. An anisogastric tube is inserted into the gastric pouch and excluded stomach to maintain the anastomosis patency. The stapler entry hole is closed with an unabsorbable barbed suture.

The surgeon resects the omega-loop jejunum and performs a side-to-side anastomosis using staples to prevent intestinal stenosis. Subsequently, the mesenteric defect is closed with a running, non-absorbable suture.

The surgeon assesses anastomotic leakage by injecting methylene blue-stained saline via an orogastric tube, reinforcing the gastrogastric anastomosis with biological glue. The contrast upper gastrointestinal study shows contrast draining well into the excluded stomach.
